frameworks.name: model-based vs model-free reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: Habitual vs planning RL as endpoints of an accuracy-effort tradeoff.

frameworks.name: metacontrol / cost-benefit arbitration
frameworks.one_line: Agent allocates between cheap and costly decision processes by weighing benefits against compute.

frameworks.name: bounded rationality / resource-rational decision making
frameworks.one_line: Computational demands enter as a registered cost on deliberation.

core_question: Do people rely more on habit when planning is computationally demanding but offers only marginal accuracy gains?
key_assumptions: Humans have access to both habitual and planning decision processes.
Decision processes embody distinct accuracy-effort tradeoffs.
Planning complexity is registered as a subjective cost during arbitration.
manipulations: Task difficulty / planning complexity across sequential RL conditions with matched rewards.
key_findings: Reliance on habitual (model-free) control increases when planning demands are higher despite equal reward for accuracy.
interpretations: People arbitrate between decision processes in a task-specific, cost-sensitive manner — hard thinking is deployed only when 'worth it.'
